metadata.title: Maximum Sum of Distinct Subarrays With Length K

learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Sliding Window, HashMap, Frequency Counting, Time Complexity
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine selecting exactly k consecutive products from a shelf. You earn points equal to the total price, but every selected product must be unique. Find the selection with the highest total value.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine selecting exactly k consecutive products from a shelf. You earn points equal to the total price, but every selected product must be unique. Find the selection with the highest total value.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use this pattern whenever a problem involves a fixed-size window with uniqueness or frequency constraints.
learning.signals: Whenever a fixed-size window requires uniqueness constraints, combine Sliding Window with a HashMap.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: You are given an integer array nums and an integer k. Find the maximum sum of any subarray of length exactly k where all elements are distinct. Return the maximum sum. If no such subarray exists, return 0.

examples.explanation: The subarray [4,2,9] contains distinct elements and has the maximum sum of 15.
No window of length 3 contains distinct elements.

edgeCases: Window at the end gives maximum sum

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: Generate every window of size k, verify whether all elements are distinct, and compute its sum.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: Since the window size is fixed, maintain a sliding window of length k. Use a HashMap to track element frequencies. A window is valid only when every element appears exactly once.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: A valid window has exactly k distinct elements.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever a fixed-size window requires uniqueness constraints, combine Sliding Window with a HashMap.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: Each element enters and leaves the sliding window exactly once. HashMap operations take O(1) on average, resulting in linear time complexity.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever a fixed-size window requires uniqueness constraints, combine Sliding Window with a HashMap.
commonWrongPaths: Using a HashSet instead of frequencies.
Not removing elements with zero frequency.
Checking only the sum without uniqueness.
Forgetting to subtract the outgoing element.
Using int instead of long for the sum.

hints: Use a HashMap to store frequencies.
A valid window has exactly k distinct elements.
commonMisconceptions: Using a HashSet instead of frequencies.
Not removing elements with zero frequency.
Checking only the sum without uniqueness.
Forgetting to subtract the outgoing element.
Using int instead of long for the sum.

pattern.name: Fixed Size Sliding Window + HashMap
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever a fixed-size window requires uniqueness constraints, combine Sliding Window with a HashMap.

approaches.title: Check Every Window
approaches.idea: Generate every window of size k, verify whether all elements are distinct, and compute its sum.
algorithm: Generate every subarray of size k.

code.java: Brute force omitted because of quadratic complexity.

approaches.title: Prefix Sum + HashSet
approaches.idea: Use prefix sums to calculate window sums quickly while checking uniqueness separately.
approaches.dryRun: Use prefix sums with a HashSet.

approaches.title: Fixed Size Sliding Window + HashMap
approaches.idea: Maintain a sliding window of size k. Track the current window sum and element frequencies using a HashMap. Whenever the window size reaches k, if the HashMap size equals k, update the answer. Then slide the window by removing the leftmost element.
approaches.intuition: Each element enters and leaves the sliding window exactly once. HashMap operations take O(1) on average, resulting in linear time complexity.
algorithm: Expand the window by adding nums[right].
If window size exceeds k, remove nums[left].
Decrease its frequency or remove it from the map.
When window size equals k and map size equals k, update the maximum sum.
approaches.dryRun:
nums=[1,5,4,2,9,9,9], k=3
Window=[1,5,4] Sum=10 ✓
Window=[5,4,2] Sum=11 ✓
Window=[4,2,9] Sum=15 ✓ Max
Window=[2,9,9] Duplicate ✗
Answer=15

code.python:
from collections import defaultdict

def maximumSubarraySum(nums, k):
    freq = defaultdict(int)
    left = 0
    total = 0
    ans = 0

    for right in range(len(nums)):
        total += nums[right]
        freq[nums[right]] += 1

        if right - left + 1 > k:
            total -= nums[left]
            freq[nums[left]] -= 1
            if freq[nums[left]] == 0:
                del freq[nums[left]]
            left += 1

        if right - left + 1 == k and len(freq) == k:
            ans = max(ans, total)

    return ans

visualization.description: Slide a fixed-size window while maintaining frequencies.
steps: Remove the left element when needed.
Check if all elements are unique.

revisionNotes: Window is valid when map size == k.

interviewFollowUps: Why use a HashMap instead of a HashSet?
Can Prefix Sum solve this optimally?
How would you return the subarray?
What if window size is variable?
How would you allow at most one duplicate?
interviewTips: Use a sliding window because k is fixed.
State O(n) time and O(k) space.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: A valid window has exactly k distinct elements.
cheatSheet.algorithm: Sliding Window → HashMap → Maintain Sum → Update Answer.

importantPoints: Map size equals k means all elements are distinct.
commonMistakes: Using a HashSet instead of frequencies.
Not removing elements with zero frequency.
Checking only the sum without uniqueness.
Forgetting to subtract the outgoing element.
Using int instead of long for the sum.
